rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical iodocyclisation of 2-allylphenols to 2-(iodomethyl)dihydrobenzofurans via in situ anodic generation of I2 and Zn(II) Lewis acid from a zinc-coated graphite electrode.
electrochemical_role: Dual anodic oxidation: first oxidation of Zn(0) coating on graphite to Zn(II) (releases Lewis acid), then anodic oxidation of iodide (via triiodide) to I2 as the on-demand iodinating species.

key_intermediates.name: Zn(II) Lewis acid (released from Zn-coated graphite anode)
transient iodosyl(aryloxy)zinc / iodophenoxy intermediate (species 2) — ArO-I tethered to Zn
HI (formed in situ; drives reverse deiodination of aryl ring)

electron_flow: At the anode, Zn(0) on the graphite electrode is first oxidised to Zn(II) (releasing a dry Lewis acid), then iodide is oxidised via I3- to I2; Zn(II) binds the deprotonated 2-allylphenol to give a bis(aryloxy)zinc complex which coordinates I2 and undergoes I–I/Zn–O metathesis to deliver an iodosyl-aryloxy species that intramolecularly iodocyclises the pendant alkene to the dihydrobenzofuran.
rate_determining_step: Formation/transfer of the transient iodosyl(aryloxy)zinc intermediate (ArO–I) that delivers I+ intramolecularly to the alkene; cyclisation of the unsubstituted (non-ring-iodinated) allylphenol is faster than that of the ring-iodinated congener.

probe_reagents.name: allylbenzene (no phenol)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction with I2/NaI or with I2/NaI/ZnCl2 — alkene alone is not iodocyclised; rules out free I+ acting on alkene.

probe_reagents.name: 2,4,6-trimethylphenol (external phenol, no tethered alkene)
probe_reagents.observation: With allylbenzene + I2/NaI/ZnCl2 + mesitol, ~10% of intermolecular iodoether 1 isolated, showing the phenol/Zn complex is required to activate iodine.

probe_reagents.name: K2CO3 (added base)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of base did not accelerate cyclisation; instead gave predominantly arene-iodinated products, arguing against a free cycloiodonium pathway and implicating the Zn-phenoxide–I intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of allylbenzene
probe_reagents.observation: No redox events within solvent window — alkene is not directly oxidised electrochemically.

probe_reagents.name: 4-iodophenol (model for ring-iodinated intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Under electrochemical conditions with MeSO3H (HI mimic), the C–I bond is labile (deiodination observed), supporting reversible aryl iodination.

probe_reagents.name: 4-iodoanisole (alkylated analogue)
probe_reagents.observation: No deiodination — C(aryl)–I in the absence of free phenol OH is not labile, showing the OH is required for the reverse deiodination equilibrium.

probe_reagents.name: MeSO3H (used to mimic HI)
probe_reagents.observation: Mimics in situ HI; together with the iodophenol experiments shows iodinated allylphenol can revert to allylphenol + I2, explaining lack of ring-iodinated by-products.

probe_reagents.name: charge–time monitoring during electrolysis
probe_reagents.observation: Two-stage charge profile: initial Zn(0)→Zn(II) oxidation, then 3 I- → I3- (-2e-), confirming the dual oxidative process of Scheme 1.

notes: Authors explicitly rule out two alternative mechanisms: (i) direct interaction of Zn(II) with I2 to generate the iodinating species (excluded by Scheme 4a–c control experiments showing alkene + I2/ZnCl2 alone is unreactive), and (ii) a free cycloiodonium-ion pathway intercepted by zinc phenoxide (excluded because added K2CO3 did not accelerate cyclisation and instead gave arene iodination). Favored pathway (Scheme 5): bis(aryloxy)zinc complex coordinates I2, undergoes I–I/Zn–O metathesis to a tethered ArO–I (iodosylaryl) species (intermediate 2), which delivers I+ intramolecularly to the allyl alkene; reversible deiodination via HI prevents accumulation of ring-iodinated arenes and the cyclised dihydrobenzofuran is itself deactivated toward further iodination, accounting for the high selectivity. No TEMPO/BHT or DFT studies were performed; mechanism is supported mainly by control experiments, CV, and charge–time electrochemical profiling.
